p.strong: The same code, different memory ordering, different outcomes

li.strong: Happens on ARM, not on x86

text.tspan: // ACQUIRE: see all prior released writes
// After acquire, x MUST be visible
// ACQUIRE: see all prior released writes
// After acquire, x MUST be visible

ul.li: Release on y ensures x is visible first
Acquire on y ensures we see x after

text.tspan: // CPU doesn't write directly to cache
// Writes go to store buffer first (~50 cycles)
// Buffer can drain in ANY order!
// Unless: RELEASE fence forces drain order
// CPU doesn't write directly to cache
// Writes go to store buffer first (~50 cycles)
// Buffer can drain in ANY order!
// Unless: RELEASE fence forces drain order
// If cache line is Shared, load is fast
// If another core has Modified, must wait

g.text: r2 = x; // Might complete before r1!

text.tspan: // If cache line is Shared, load is fast
// If another core has Modified, must wait

div.p: Transitivity: writer.w1 → writer.w2 → reader.r1 → reader.r2 Therefore: writer.w1 happens-before reader.r2 Reader MUST see all data writes!
